title: A Prayer of the Godly
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: A Prayer of the Godly - Pastor Tito Lyro - Psalm 12.txt

outline.title: A Prayer of Deliverance (Psalm 12:1-4)
subpoints: The Psalm begins with the simplest and most essential prayer: Help, Lord
The Psalmist turns to the only one who can help, not away from God like Elijah, but toward him
The evidence he brings before God is the perversion of speech in society — everyone speaking flattery and with a double heart
The implication: the godly should use the tongue differently; when the godly stay silent, it seems as though the wicked have taken over

outline.title: The Problem of Lying Tongues and the Silence of the Godly
subpoints: Society loudly proclaims lies and the godly are silent — we pick our battles, which is code for never fighting any battle
LGBTQ/transgender ideology amounts to less than 1% of the population yet dominates public discourse; 33% of Americans who profess Christianity are quiet
James 3:10-12: a spring cannot send forth both fresh and bitter water — the godly cannot be double-tongued
The catechism teaches that silence in the face of a lie is itself lying; letting cultural norms go unchallenged is to be complicit in the lie

outline.title: God's Answer to the Prayer of the Godly (Psalm 12:5)
subpoints: For the oppression of the poor, for the sighing of the needy, now I will arise, says the Lord — God answers the prayers of his people
The fact that verse 5 is present reassures us that God answers the prayers of the desperate
Even when God's answer seems delayed, he may be answering in the way that is best for us and brings the greatest glory to himself
God's promise is not necessarily peace but presence — he will never leave or forsake the godly, and that is enough

outline.title: Confidence in the Word of God (Psalm 12:6-7)
subpoints: The words of the Lord are pure words, like silver refined in a furnace, purified seven times — perfectly purified
The Psalmist's despair dissolves when he remembers God's trustworthy word: You will keep them, Lord, You will preserve them
Right thinking about who God is and what he is doing is the cure for depression and despair — not changed circumstances
The joy of the Lord has more to do with our interaction with our Savior than with the circumstances of our lives (Philippians 4:8-9)

outline.title: How the Godly Should Act: Speaking Truth in a Lying Culture
subpoints: Ephesians 4:25: put away lying, let each one speak truth with his neighbor, for we are members of one another
We speak truth not only with words but with how we work — stealing by laziness, not just by taking; corrupt speech includes silence
Verse 32: be kind to one another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another — bitterness in the church is itself a form of lying
We are to be tenderhearted and thick-skinned in this culture: Lord, You take vengeance; I will love and speak truth

summary:
Pastor Tito Lyro preaches from Psalm 12, a Psalm without a specific historical occasion, which he describes as an expression of the general experience of the godly in every age. The Psalm moves from despair to joy without any change in external circumstances — the wicked are equally present at the end as at the beginning. What changes is not the situation but the Psalmist's thinking: he turns to God, remembers God's promises, and his perspective is transformed.

The sermon identifies the opening as a pattern for the godly in every culture: Help, Lord. When lies are spoken loudly and the faithful are silent, the world feels as though the godly have disappeared. Pastor Lyro draws an extended parallel to the present moment in America, where ideologies representing a tiny fraction of the population dominate public discourse precisely because those who know the truth have stayed quiet. The Psalmist's charge is both diagnosis and call: we are lying when we fail to speak what is true, and God hates lying.

The second major movement of the sermon is Ephesians 4:25-32, which Pastor Lyro reads as Paul's practical instruction for how to speak truth in a lying culture. The passage covers truthful speech, righteous anger, honest work, edifying words, and the absence of bitterness. Bitterness in the church — holding grudges, refusing forgiveness — is itself a form of lying. The person who is bitter is acting as if Christ's forgiveness does not apply, and this too is how the godly testify to or against the truth.

The sermon closes by affirming the Psalmist's pattern: the only thing that changed between verse 1 and verse 8 was how the Psalmist thought about God. He went from depression to joy without the wicked going anywhere. Peace and joy are found in Christ, not in circumstances. Meditating on whatever is true, noble, just, pure, lovely, and of good report — Philippians 4:8-9 — is the path to experiencing the God of peace.
transcript:
If you're able, you remain standing and turn to Psalm 12. Return to our series on 1st John, Lord willing, in the next Lord's day. Psalm 12, read the whole Psalm. This is the Word of our Lord.

*To the chief musician on an eight-stringed harp, a Psalm of David. Help, Lord, for the godly man ceases, for the faithful disappear from among the sons of men. They speak idly everyone with his neighbor; with flattering lips and a double heart they speak. May the Lord cut off all flattering lips and the tongue that speaks proud things, who have said, with our tongue we will prevail; our lips are our own; who is lord over us? For the oppression of the poor, for the sighing of the needy, now I will arise, says the Lord; I will set him in the safety for which he yearns. The words of the Lord are pure words, like silver tried in a furnace of earth, purified seven times. You shall keep them, Lord, You shall preserve them from this generation forever. The wicked prowl on every side, when vileness is exalted among the sons of men.*

This is the Word of our Lord. Let us pray together. Father, thank You for Your Word. We pray Father that You speak to us as we look into it. We pray our eyes will be open to see Your glory, for asking Jesus name. Amen, please be seated.

If you look at the title of the Psalm, it does not tell us anything about the occasion. It is not a Psalm that is attached to any particular time in the life of David — he just tells us what instrument it is supposed to be used and what kind of song it is. It is really an expression in general of the experience of the godly person. And Psalm 12, I think we can find all of our experiences in everyday life in it.

The Psalm goes from despair and depression generated by the overwhelming presence of the wicked and the wickedness, and by the seeming absence of the godly in society, to trusting God and trusting his promise, and to a biblically informed view of the world. And we often find ourselves in that cycle where we get despair, we fall into despair, we get depressed because of everything that is going on around us. Then we start thinking about the Lord, we think about what he has done for us. And without any change in the circumstances around us, we are able to overcome that despair, we overcome that depression and experience the joy of the Lord when we train our thinking and our acting.

Do you notice that verse 1 and verse 8 are almost identical? Meaning there is no difference in the circumstances of the Psalmist when you look at the beginning and the end of the Psalm. The wicked are still around, wickedness is still very present around him. And yet he has changed his attitude. We can see the progression of faith presented here by David as the experience of the godly. Starts in despair in verse 1, then he prays in verses 1 through 3, then he utters a curse upon the wicked in verses 3 and 4, then God reminds him of his promises in verse 5, then confidence in the Word of God comes up in his heart in verses 6 and 7, and the Psalm ends with an accurate understanding of the world in verse 8.

In all of this there are no changes in the circumstances of the godly, only in his thinking and in his actions. It was right thinking and acting that changed the godly person's perspective. And we often get it backwards in our lives. We get depressed because of the circumstances around us. Then we wait for things around us to change in order for us to come out of that depression. We tether our joy to everything else that is going on around us. And then we wait for those things to change in order to drag us out of whatever it is that we are in.

We see that we do that in our marriages. We know what is right but we think that there must be something else, and just thinking and acting in a certain way cannot be it. There must be a secret and until I find this elusive secret I am going to be in despair. We may not formulate those thoughts exactly like that but we often act in that way. We do that in life in general. We get depressed because of the blank in the sentence: if only blank I would be happy. And the problem is that joy is not in that. It is in an accurate view of God and his Word and of the world around us.

There are four parts in this song that we are going to use to look at it.

## Part One: A Prayer of Deliverance (verses 1-4)

The first part is a prayer of deliverance in verses 1 through 4. The first thing the Psalmist does is to turn to the only one who can help him. You see how the song begins? Help, Lord. That is a prayer that we all can pray. You know, whatever it is in our lives, whatever is going on — Help, Lord. And this has to be the first and foremost thing in our lives when we deal with every issue. Help, Lord. We must go to God because he is the source of all help. And he will help us, as evidence in verse 5 when the Psalmist prayed to him in verse 5 God says, I will stand for the Godly. That is the testimony of the Psalmist throughout the Psalm. For example, in Psalm 46:1-3, the Psalmist says, God is our refuge and strength, a very present help in trouble; therefore we will not fear.

In this particular instance, the Psalmist turns to the Lord because his circumstances indicate that the godly and the faithful were disappearing. Verse 1 again says, help Lord for the godly man ceases, for the faithful disappear from among the sons of men. I think the Psalmist is using hyperbolic language, which is a valid way to express thought. It does not mean that literally there is no godly, there is no faithful around him. But the presence of the wicked people around him and the lack of relevance or impact of the godly on society made it seem like there were no godly people in that society.

You ever felt that way — that you are the only one left, that there is nothing there, that everything is so wicked and it feels like no matter what you do there is no hope, that you just beat your head against a wall? That is the experience of the Psalmist. And often that happens because godly people keep their mouths shut. I find that in myself. I was in Brazil just one night spending time with unbelieving friends from high school — flaming liberals and all kinds of false ideas — and I often found myself staying quiet instead of saying something. And we find ourselves doing that quite often. I think we say, oh we need to pick our battles. That is code for never fighting a battle.

And often it feels like everything is going to hell in a handbasket because we are quiet, because we do not stand for righteousness, and the wicked is loud. Think about LGBTQ and transgenderism: in the United States it amounts to less than 1% of the total population. And you would think it was 99% of the population by the way it is loudly spoken about. And yet you and I, who supposedly account for 33% of the American population, are quiet. So it feels like everything is going to hell in a handbasket because you and I have decided that we do not have to speak up for the Lord.

So the Psalmist says, help, Lord. And notice that the attitude of the Psalmist is very different from that of Elijah. Remember Elijah in 1 Kings 18-19: you have the great victory over the prophets of Baal. All the 450 prophets of Baal are killed. He pronounces a drought on the land that comes to pass. And then Jezebel says I am going to get you. And Elijah runs and runs and starts sulking. He says, I am the only one left. Nobody understands me. God asked me to do all these things and yet there is nothing to be done, because I am the only one left. Poor me. And meanwhile God is feeding him, giving him something to drink, giving him rest. And God comes to him and speaks to him. And what does God say? He does not say, now now Elijah it is going to be okay, let me give you a hug. No, he says, you need to go anoint whoever the king is going to be in Israel. Go do that. And by the way Elijah, there are at least 7,000 in Israel who have not bowed their knees to Baal.

But that is not the attitude you find in the Psalm. The Psalmist found himself in a similar situation where it seemed like the godly had perished. What does he do? Does he run away from God and complain like Elijah did? No, he does the opposite. He runs to God because he is the only one that can do something about it. Help, Lord.

And the evidence that the Psalmist brings before God is the use of the tongue by those around him. Look at verse 2: they speak idly everyone with his neighbor with flattery lips and a double heart they speak. The evidence that the godly is disappearing is that the speech of society is such that it displeases God. There is no one speaking what is right. There is no one saying the things that God says in society.

And notice that the assumption of the Psalmist, and of the Scriptures in general, is that this way of using the tongue in verse 2 is a characteristic of the wicked. And the implication then is that the godly should not use his or her tongue in this way. Now it is well known by us that when the Bible talks about the tongue, it is not talking about this muscle — it is talking about our speech that reflects the heart. The Psalmist says as much in verse 2: they speak idly everyone with his neighbor with flattery lips and a double heart they speak.

The particular speech pattern the Psalmist is referring to here is the perversion and twisting of the truth — otherwise known as lying. And he says no one is standing up to speak the truth. Society and culture are saying what is a lie very loudly, and those who know the truth are staying quiet.

There is no way we cannot relate to that where we live today. How many times during these last couple of years with all that is being said, have we stayed quiet — just because it is embarrassing to be always the odd man out, embarrassing to be the one that supposedly does not believe in science. Every time somebody says that, I think of a certain character in a wrestling show who says, I do not believe in God, I believe in science. It is such a foolish statement, as if you cannot be a person of faith and also a person of science. But that is the problem. Society in Psalm 12 is speaking lies. And they are speaking loudly and everyone is accepting that as the norm.

So it appears like there is no godly, only wicked. And the Psalmist says, Lord, help me — for there are only lies being spoken and the godly is quiet. The ungodly are portrayed as wanting to gain power by flattery, by deception, by clever schemes. And the source of their deceit is clearly pointed out in verse 2 — it is the heart. The ungodly are one thing in their heart but something else in their speech. They are double-hearted, double-tongued, which is to say they are hypocrites. And the Bible says that the godly, those who are united to the Lord Jesus Christ, cannot be that.

In James chapter 3 verse 10 and 12, James says, out of the same mouth come blessing and cursing. My brethren, these things ought not to be so. Does a spring send forth fresh water and bitter from the same opening? Can a fig tree, my brethren, bear olives, or a grapevine bear figs? Thus no spring yields both salt water and fresh. Godly springs do what? They spring water, right? You put a plug in it and it will come out. No matter what, what is it going to come out? Truth or not?

Our catechism says that when we do not speak the truth, guess what we are doing? We are lying. It does not matter that we are not saying words. If you are not putting forth the truth, you are lying. And when we let cultural norms go unchallenged as the church of Jesus Christ, we are lying. And when we buy into them, we are lying. Lying is a characteristic of the ungodly, not of the godly. And God hates the liar. In Proverbs 6:16-19, it says, these six things the Lord hates, yes, seven are an abomination to Him. A proud look, a lying tongue, hands that shed innocent blood, a heart that devises wicked plans, feet that are swift in running to evil, a false witness who speaks lies, and one who sows discord among brethren.

## Part Two: God Answers (verse 5)

And the Lord answers the prayers of the godly. Look at verse 5. For the oppression of the poor, for the sighing of the needy, now I will arise, says the Lord. I will set him in the safety for which he yearns. The just fact that verse 5 is here reassures us once again that God answers the prayers of his people. You might say, I have been praying for this or that and I have not seen any answer. My answer to that is that while we may think that God has not answered our prayers, he may have answered our prayer by doing what is best for us and brings the greatest glory to himself.

One of the most shocking, difficult-to-accept passages is in Hebrews chapter 2, where the Holy Spirit says that the Father delivered the Son through death. It is by dying that God answered the prayer of Jesus not to die — because then on the third day he came back to life. And all our prayers for peace and harmony will be answered. But we may have to wait until the day of the return of the Lord Jesus Christ. The Lord is going to continue to be faithful to his promises and his promise is to keep the godly in his hands. It is to never leave him and never forsake him. Not a promise of perfect peace in this age, but a promise of presence.

And as the Psalmist realizes that, he goes from despair into joy.

## Part Three: Confidence in the Word of God (verses 6-7)

Look at verses 6 and 7. The words of the Lord are pure words, like silver tried in a furnace of earth, purified seven times. You shall keep them, Lord. You shall preserve them from this generation forever.

All of a sudden the Psalmist realizes, oh, Your Word is trustworthy. You tell me that You are keeping me. You tell me that You are working in me. Your Word is being proved right — purified seven times, perfectly purified. And I can trust in that. Thank You, Lord, for pointing me back to Your Word so I can see who You are and what You are doing and see Your attributes present. I know that the Word of God is trustworthy.

The Psalmist regains his joy because God says He will keep His people. In verse 7, the word them refers back to the godly. And the idea is that the wicked may turn this world upside down, but God will protect his own. Even if that protection happens through death, because the resurrection is coming. The wicked will be around, but so will be God.

Look at verse 8. The wicked prowl on every side when vileness is exalted among the sons of men. As long as vileness is honored among men the wicked will be around. But now the Psalmist does not despair because his thinking is right and he knows that God is also around. Same reality as verse 1, and yet now he is thinking rightly about who God is and what God is doing.

So we see a progression in the thinking of the Psalmist. He goes from depression to joy without any changes in circumstances. The only thing that changed was how he thought about God. And this is true for us as well. I would like to suggest to you that having the joy of the Lord has much more to do with our thinking about God than anything that goes on around us. The joy of the Lord has more to do with our interaction with our Savior than the circumstances of our lives.

Think again with me of Philippians 4:8-9. Paul says, finally brethren, whatever things are true, whatever things are noble, whatever things are just, whatever things are pure, whatever things are lovely, whatever things are of good report, if there is any virtue and if there is anything praiseworthy, meditate on these things. And what is the result? The things which you learned and received and heard and saw in me, these do, and the God of peace will be with you.

What is more true or noble or more just or pure or lovely or of good report than the person and the work of the Lord Jesus Christ? Peace and joy go hand in hand. They are both found in following Jesus Christ, not in our circumstances.

## Part Four: How the Godly Responds — Speaking Truth in a Lying Culture

So what do we do? Well, we think God's thoughts after him. We are able to discipline our thought life. In a society and a culture where lies prevail, we think what is correct, we think what is right, we think God's thoughts after him. But also we act the way God calls us to act.

Keep your finger or something there in Psalm 12 and turn to Ephesians 4 for a second. Look at verse 25 and let us read all the way to the end of the chapter. We act the way God calls us. We think God's thoughts after him. And in a society that is rejoicing in lies, we act the way that God calls us to act.

*Therefore, putting away lying, Let each one of you speak truth with his neighbor, for we are members of one another. Be angry, and do not sin: do not let the sun go down on your wrath, nor give place to the devil. Let him who stole steal no longer, but rather let him labor, working with his hands what is good, that he may have something to give him who has need. Let no corrupt word proceed out of your mouth, but what is good for necessary edification, that it may impart grace to the hearers. And do not grieve the Holy Spirit of God, by whom you were sealed for the day of redemption. Let all bitterness, wrath, anger, clamor, and evil speaking be put away from you, with all malice. And be kind to one another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, even as God in Christ forgave you.*

Notice the first thing he says in verse 25: put away lying. Let each one of you speak truth with his neighbor, for we are members of one another. The godly does not just not speak lies to be a godly person — you also speak truth.

And he continues in verse 28: let him who stole steal no longer, but rather let him labor, working with his hands what is good, that he may have something to give him who has need. As we speak truth in society, we do that by being honest in everything that we do but not stealing. We can steal by being lazy, we can steal by not using the time at work the way we are supposed to, we can steal by not being productive. All these things are ways that we are speaking either truth or lies to the culture that we are in.

And he continues in verse 29: let no corrupt word proceed out of your mouth but what is good for necessary edification, that it may impart grace to the hearers. I think God is not big into small talk. He wants our words to be intentional and purposeful, to accomplish something — and that something is the good of everyone who is around us.

But we are proud people. We do not want to be thought less of. So when it would be good to say something, when it would be good for someone to hear the truth, we tend to hold back because we are afraid of how society is going to see us. Because they are going to think of us as a certain kind of person. And that is also our fault, because as a church of Jesus Christ we have sometimes made ourselves into a political brand. Let no corrupt word proceed out of our mouth but what is needed, what people around us need to hear. We are the agents God is using to build up those people around us.

Verse 30: and do not grieve the Holy Spirit of God by whom you were sealed for the day of redemption. How do we grieve the Spirit? By doing exactly the opposite of what Paul is saying here.

Verses 31-32: let all bitterness, wrath, anger, clamor, and evil speaking be put away from you, with all malice. And be kind to one another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, even as God in Christ forgave you. Here Paul grabs a bunch of words that are near-synonyms, just slightly different from one another, and matches them all together so that there is no room in our thinking for any idea that bitterness is acceptable in the church of Jesus Christ. One of the ways that we speak the truth of the society we are in is by having relationships in the church of Jesus Christ that are marked by forgiveness, reconciliation, and lack of bitterness.

So right now if you are bitter toward somebody, you are a liar. You are not acting as a child of God. But you do not know what they did to me. Do you know what you did to Christ? And he chose to forgive you. If you are truly forgiven, then bitterness cannot be present in your life.

If you want godliness to thrive around you, it is not just because bad people are out there. It is also because of the things in our own hearts that need to be eliminated. Verse 32: be kind to one another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, even as God in Christ forgave you. Tenderhearted and thick-skinned — that is what we need to have in this culture.

And notice back in Psalm 12, the Psalmist's attitude toward the sins going on around him in society. May the Lord, verse 3, may the Lord cut off all flattering lips and the tongue that speaks proud things. In essence, he is saying: Lord, You take vengeance. I will be tenderhearted. I will speak the truth. I will minister. I will build up. I will say what needs to be said. You vindicate me. I will love and You vindicate, Lord. And we have the same attitude, much like Romans 12: I will love, You vindicate.

And the Lord answers the prayers of the godly, look at verse 5. The just fact that verse 5 is here reassures us once again that God is going to work, that God has heard. Even when we think God has not answered our prayers, he may have answered our prayer by doing what is best for us. Our prayers for peace and harmony will be answered. But it may be that we have to wait until the day of the return of the Lord Jesus Christ.

So brothers and sisters, friends — think rightly about God. Speak the truth. God is going to vindicate you. Peace and joy are found in the person and work of the Lord Jesus Christ, not in our circumstances.

Let us pray together. Father, thank You for Your Word. We thank You for the experience of the Psalmist — our experience. We thank You that we can read about the things that we deal with in Your Word, even though this was written 3,000 years ago. Father, pray that we would seek help from You. In a society and a culture where lies prevail, we would be willing to speak the truth in love for the edification of all that are around us. We are asking in Jesus name. Amen.
